author: @Vesalempinen
text: I Thank you for this. I use FreeCAD regurarly, and was wondering what would be the best way to make a fusee  part for ship chronometer that I am building at the moment. Vintage working Fusee machines are scarce and very expensive antiquities to obtain.

author: @ZzZSKyFauX
text:
You ought use the works of Ken Lee Wheeler/Theoria Apophasis to update your vocabulary.  The "lines of force" thing is from academicians that use many words to say nothing, like Feyman and Einstein.  Ken's papers will supercharge your magnet and matter skills.  I keep referring to him your tetrataenite video.  You two should talk about minimum necessary steps to make programmed tetrataenite forms.  He's trying to source pyramidal magnets en masse for a device, but the tariffs with China messed up his order.  
You'll appreciate a lot of his stuff.

author: @benjaminwelborn8104
text: Magnetic force follows the inverse cubed law... not squared. Thats why (ive been told) we cant set up  magnetic orbits...

replies.author: @LoganKearsley
replies.text: Magnetic monopoles produce an inverse-square field, but there are no isolated magnetic monopoles, and dipole fields follow a cube law. There are no stable orbits in an inverse cube force field, but the fact that it's a dipole field is a bigger problem when trying to get real magnets to orbit each other!

replies.author: @TheZitherish
replies.text: Add an opposite pole magnet to the follow through side.

author: @victorro8760
text: I've heard that magnetic force falls of with the cube of the distance. I tried to look it up to confirm but it went way over my head.

replies.author: @chris993361
replies.text: Nah. It's an inverse square rule.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: square of the distance mate

replies.author: @LoganKearsley
replies.text: An ideal dipole field drops in strength as the inverse cube. That's fairly trivial to derive as the limit of two monopole fields. But real magnets have spatial extent are not perfect dipoles in the near field, and two interacting magnets make for a more complex field. So in practice, you'll be better of just measuring the actual force curve for a particular setup than trying to calculate it most of the time.

author: @Bill-n9l7d
text: I have a 3 d printer on my to get list. Meanwhile I think I'll make a model of the catapult of scrap wood I have just for fun. I wonder if maybe there are some hidden design features of the catapult he encoded in the journal that no one understands? It might be he was worried someone would steal his inventions. Something to ponder on I suppose. I have good news and bad news I conducted an experiment and it didn't go the way I'd hoped for so. Back to the drawing board.  The good news is I did away with a theory I'm happy. I'm learning. I just thought of something that would be cool but what if it were possible to do those magnetic lines like they do light with a polarization lens. We'll have to get spock on the computers. 😊

replies.author: @Bill-n9l7d
replies.text: What if you redesigned the catapult to have four leaf springs inside of the original two. The arm swings back and forth until it equalizes then" I think I said that right" runs out of energy. Put a stone on it and you'd still have a problem. However if you give an extra wrap or two between the two correct spring leafs the stone would be ejected maybe but the arm probably will not swing back to kick into the trigger. So maybe you might solve that problem with a string leaf catching the arm at the right place helping the arm go back to lock. I'm going to build one out of wood I'm slow 😊
